message:
feat(fsfs,index): query execution and FSVI improvements, update beads

- Expand query execution infrastructure in frankensearch-fsfs.
- Further FSVI index hardening in frankensearch-index.
- Sync beads issue tracker state.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Delivered scope:
1) Prioritized ingest/crawl hotspots + target gains
- Added crawl/ingest optimization matrix + ranked track contract in `crates/frankensearch-fsfs/src/profiling.rs`
- New public contract types:
  - `CrawlIngestStage`
  - `CrawlIngestHotspot`
  - `CrawlIngestOptimizationTrack`
  - `IsomorphismProofChecklistItem`
  - `RollbackGuardrail`
- New builders:
  - `crawl_ingest_opportunity_matrix()`
  - `crawl_ingest_optimization_track()`

2) Isomorphism proof checklist per optimization lever
- Added explicit baseline comparators, invariant sets, and replay commands per stage/lever in `profiling.rs`.

3) Rollback guardrails per optimization class
- Added deterministic rollback commands + abort reason-code sets + expected recovery reason-code per stage/lever in `profiling.rs`.

4) Integration + docs alignment
- Exported new contracts in `crates/frankensearch-fsfs/src/lib.rs`
- Added integration assertions in `crates/frankensearch-fsfs/tests/profiling_harness_workflow.rs`
- Documented the final hotspot/proof/rollback track in `docs/fsfs-alien-recommendation-contracts.md`

Validation (all cargo invocations via `rch exec`):
- `cargo test -p frankensearch-fsfs --test profiling_harness_workflow -- --nocapture` ✅ (5 passed)
- `cargo check -p frankensearch-fsfs --lib` ✅
- `cargo clippy -p frankensearch-fsfs --lib --no-deps -- -D warnings` ✅
- `cargo fmt --check` ⚠️ blocked by pre-existing unrelated formatting deltas in other files
- `cargo check -p frankensearch-fsfs --all-targets` ⚠️ blocked by unrelated existing errors in other active-agent surfaces:
  - `crates/frankensearch-tui/src/shell.rs` missing `ratatui::layout` imports
  - `crates/frankensearch-fsfs/src/cli_e2e.rs` test-scope `ArtifactEntry` resolution issue (file reserved by VioletBeaver)

Additional formatting validation for touched Rust files:
- `rustfmt --edition 2024 --check crates/frankensearch-fsfs/src/profiling.rs crates/frankensearch-fsfs/src/lib.rs crates/frankensearch-fsfs/tests/profiling_harness_workflow.rs` ✅

LOGGING_ARTIFACTS: For bd-2hz.9.5, require structured logs/traces and reproducible artifact references (manifest path, replay command, CI job link).","created_at":"2026-02-14T08:28:20Z"},{"id":1435,"issue_id":"bd-2hz.9.5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Implementation: TUI Frame Pipeline Optimization

### Changes Made (SapphireMoose)

#### 1. FramePipelineTimer (frame.rs)
Three-phase latency timer aligned with TUI latency boundaries:
- `InputToIntent` (8ms budget) — event receipt to action resolution
- `IntentToState` (12ms budget) — action dispatch to state mutation
- `StateToFrame` (16ms budget) — state read to rendered frame
Includes `skip_input_begin_state()` for timer/data-driven frames.
`FramePipelineMetrics` struct reports per-phase durations and budget conformance.

#### 2. CachedLayout (frame.rs)
Eliminates redundant `Layout::split()` calls on every frame render.
Caches computed layout chunks keyed on (area, breadcrumbs_visible, status_bar, screen_count).
Only recomputes when any input parameter changes (typically on terminal resize).

#### 3. CachedTabState (frame.rs)
Caches tab titles and selected index using hash-based invalidation.
Avoids rebuilding `Vec<Line>` titles and linear-searching selected index every frame.
Invalidated automatically on navigation via `AppShell::navigate_to()`.

#### 4. Shell Integration (shell.rs)
- `AppShell::render()` changed from `&self` to `&mut self` to enable caching
- Uses `CachedLayout::get_or_compute()` instead of raw `Layout::split()`
- Uses `CachedTabState` for tab rendering with hash-based freshness check
- `navigate_to()` invalidates tab cache when active screen changes

#### 5. Downstream Updates
- `OpsApp::render()` updated to `&mut self` (only caller of `shell.render()`)

#### Test Coverage
- 14 new tests covering all optimization components
- All 145 frankensearch-tui tests pass
- Clippy clean (-D warnings), rustfmt clean
- All consumer crates (fsfs, ops) compile clean

#### Flicker Prevention
- Layout cache prevents unnecessary constraint recalculation
- Tab cache prevents title string allocations when navigation hasn't changed
- `TerminalState::needs_full_redraw()` dirty tracking already exists and composes with cached layout

 {"id":"bd-qtx","title":"Implement quantization ladder (f32/f16/int8/int4) with formal quality bounds","description":"Implement a quantization ladder (f32/f16/int8/int4) with formal quality-memory tradeoff characterization for vector indices. Enables extreme memory savings for large-scale deployments.

OPTIMIZATION RATIONALE:

Current FSVI format supports f32 and f16. For large indices (100K+ docs), memory is the bottleneck:
- 100K docs x 384 dims x f32 = 147 MB
- 100K docs x 384 dims x f16 = 73 MB   (current default)
- 100K docs x 384 dims x int8 = 37 MB   (THIS BEAD)
- 100K docs x 384 dims x int4 = 18 MB   (THIS BEAD)

Opportunity Matrix:
| Quantization | Memory | Quality Loss | Effort | Score (Impact x Conf / Effort) |
|-------------|--------|-------------|--------|-------------------------------|
| int8 scalar | 4x vs f32 | ~1-2%  | 2      | 10.0                          |
| int4 packed | 8x vs f32 | ~3-5%  | 4      | 5.0                           |

IMPLEMENTATION:

1. Scalar Quantization (int8):
   - Per-dimension min/max computed during indexing
   - Quantize: q = round((x - min) / (max - min) * 255)
   - Dequantize: x' = q / 255 * (max - min) + min
   - Store quantization parameters in FSVI header (2 floats per dimension)
   - SIMD dot product on int8: use i16 multiply-accumulate via wide crate

pub struct ScalarQuantizer {
    mins: Vec<f32>,     // Per-dimension minimum
    scales: Vec<f32>,   // Per-dimension (max - min) / 255
}

impl ScalarQuantizer {
    pub fn fit(vectors: &[Vec<f32>]) -> Self;
    pub fn quantize(&self, vector: &[f32]) -> Vec<u8>;
    pub fn dot_product_quantized(&self, stored: &[u8], query: &[f32]) -> f32;
}

2. Product Quantization (optional, for int4-equivalent compression):
   - Split 384-dim vector into 48 sub-vectors of 8 dims each
   - K-means cluster each sub-space into 256 centroids
   - Store 1 byte per sub-vector (centroid index)
   - Asymmetric distance computation (ADC): query in full precision, database quantized
   - Compression: 384 dims -> 48 bytes (8x vs f32, 4x vs f16)

3. FSVI Format Extension:
   - quantization field in header: 0=f32, 1=f16, 2=int8, 3=int4/PQ
   - For int8: append quantization parameters after header (mins + scales)
   - For PQ: append codebook after header

4. Quality Characterization (Alien-Artifact):
   - For each quantization level, compute NDCG@10 on the test fixture corpus
   - Provide formal bounds on maximum quality loss:
     For int8: |cos_sim(q, x) - cos_sim(q, x')| <= epsilon
     where epsilon = max_dim(scale_i / 255) * sqrt(d) / ||q|| / ||x||
   - This is a PROVABLE bound on the worst-case quality degradation

5. Automatic Selection:
   pub fn recommended_quantization(index_size: usize, available_memory: usize) -> Quantization {
       let f16_size = index_size * dimension * 2;
       let int8_size = index_size * dimension;
       if f16_size <= available_memory { Quantization::F16 }
       else if int8_size <= available_memory { Quantization::Int8 }
       else { Quantization::PQ }
   }

","created_at":"2026-02-13T20:31:32Z"},{"id":244,"issue_id":"bd-qtx","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"REVIEW FIX — Quality bound formula correction, naming, and missing deps:

1. QUALITY BOUND FORMULA CORRECTION: The body states:
   |cos_sim(q, x) - cos_sim(q, x')| <= epsilon
   where epsilon = max_dim(scale_i / 255) * sqrt(d) / ||q|| / ||x||

   This is INCORRECT. The correct bound:
   - Quantization error per dimension: |x_i - x'_i| <= scale_i / 255
   - Vector error: ||x - x'|| <= sqrt(sum((scale_i/255)²))
   - Dot product error: |dot(q, x) - dot(q, x')| <= ||q|| * ||x - x'||
   - Cosine similarity error:
     |cos_sim(q, x) - cos_sim(q, x')| <= ||x - x'|| / ||x|| ≈ sqrt(sum((scale_i/255)²)) / ||x||

   For unit-normalized vectors (||x|| = 1, typical for embeddings):
     epsilon ≈ sqrt(sum((scale_i/255)²))

   For uniform scale across dimensions (scale = max - min):
     epsilon ≈ (scale / 255) * sqrt(d)

   For 384-dim unit vectors with typical scale ~2: epsilon ≈ (2/255) * sqrt(384) ≈ 0.154
   This means int8 quantization introduces at most ~15% cosine similarity error — verifiable empirically.

2. NAMING FIX: "int4-equivalent" for Product Quantization is MISLEADING. PQ stores 1 byte per sub-vector (centroid index), not 4-bit integers. Rename:

   pub enum QuantizationLevel {
       F32,                        // Full precision (4 bytes/dim)
       F16,                        // Half precision (2 bytes/dim) — current default
       Int8 { scale: f32, zero: f32 },  // Scalar quantization (1 byte/dim)
       ProductQuantization {       // PQ: sub-vector centroid indices
           n_subvectors: usize,    // Default: 48 (for 384-dim)
           n_centroids: usize,     // Default: 256 (1 byte per index)
       },
   }

3. WIDE CRATE INT SIMD: The `wide` crate supports f32x8 and i32x8 but NOT i16 or i8 multiply-accumulate. For int8 dot product with accumulation into i32:
   - Option A: Use i32x8 from `wide` (widen i8 to i32, then multiply-accumulate) — safe, portable
   - Option B: Use Rust nightly std::simd for native i8 operations — nightly-only
   - DECISION: Use Option A (i32x8 widening) for V1. It's 4x slower than native i8 SIMD but safe and portable. Profile before optimizing.

4. MISSING DEPENDENCIES — Add:
   - bd-3un.38 (test corpus for NDCG evaluation)
   - bd-3un.5 (VectorHit result types)
   - bd-3un.2 (SearchError for error handling)

5. DEPENDENCY TYPE FIX: bd-3un should be parent-child, not blocks.

6. TEST REQUIREMENTS:
   - Int8 round-trip: quantize f32 → int8 → dequantize, max error within bound
   - NDCG regression: NDCG@10(int8) >= 0.95 * NDCG@10(f16) on test corpus
   - PQ round-trip: train centroids, quantize, dequantize, max error within expected range
   - FSVI format: write/read int8 quantized index (format version check)
   - Auto-selection: given memory budget, correct quantization level chosen
   - Edge cases: zero vectors, constant vectors, single-dimension vectors
   - Int8 dot product: matches f32 reference within quantization tolerance
   - Memory savings: verify 4x reduction for int8 vs f32, 2x vs f16","created_at":"2026-02-13T21:50:40Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-sot","title":"Soft-Delete Tombstones in FSVI","description":"Implement soft-delete tombstones in the FSVI vector index format. Currently, deleting a document requires a full index rebuild. Tombstones allow O(1) logical deletion by flipping a bit in the record's flags field.

## Background

The FSVI format stores document embeddings as fixed-size records with a header containing metadata fields. Each record already has a `flags: u16` field that is currently reserved (always zero). This bead repurposes bit 0 of the flags field as a tombstone marker, enabling O(1) logical deletion without rewriting the index file.

Document deletion is a basic operation that currently requires an expensive full rebuild. For mcp_agent_mail_rust (message archival with TTL), messages expire continuously, and rebuilding the entire index for each expiration is impractical. For xf, users may want to remove specific tweets from search results without re-indexing.

## Design

### Tombstone Flag

```rust
const TOMBSTONE_FLAG: u16 = 0x0001;  // Bit 0 of the flags field
```

The tombstone is set by writing a single u16 to the record's flags offset in the memory-mapped file. This is an atomic write on all supported platforms (u16 is naturally aligned in the FSVI record layout).

### API

```rust
impl VectorIndex {
    pub fn soft_delete(&mut self, doc_id: &str) -> Result<bool, SearchError>;
    pub fn soft_delete_batch(&mut self, doc_ids: &[&str]) -> Result<usize, SearchError>;
    pub fn is_deleted(&self, record_index: usize) -> bool;
    pub fn tombstone_count(&self) -> usize;
    pub fn tombstone_ratio(&self) -> f64;  // tombstones / total records
    pub fn needs_vacuum(&self) -> bool;    // tombstone_ratio > threshold (default: 0.2)
    pub fn vacuum(&mut self) -> Result<VacuumStats, SearchError>;
}

pub struct VacuumStats {
    pub records_before: usize,
    pub records_after: usize,
    pub tombstones_removed: usize,
    pub bytes_reclaimed: usize,
    pub duration: Duration,
}
```

### Search Integration

During top-k scan, skip records with the tombstone flag set:
```rust
if self.is_deleted(record_index) {
    continue;  // Skip tombstoned record
}
```

This adds a single bit-test per record — negligible overhead (< 1ns per record). The BinaryHeap-based top-k selection naturally handles the reduced effective record count.

### Vacuum

When tombstone_ratio exceeds the threshold (default: 0.2, meaning 20% of records are deleted), vacuum rewrites the index without tombstoned records:
1. Scan all records, collecting non-tombstoned ones
2. Write to `.fsvi.new` file
3. Atomic `rename()` over the old file (same pattern as compaction)

Vacuum is semantically identical to compaction — both rewrite the index file. When WAL support is implemented (Idea 1), compaction and vacuum can be unified: compaction merges main + WAL and removes tombstones in a single pass.

### Interaction with WAL (Idea 1)

- WAL entries use the same flags field and can be tombstoned with the same mechanism
- Compaction naturally removes tombstoned entries from both main index and WAL
- A document appended via WAL and then soft-deleted will be tombstoned in the WAL, and the tombstone will be dropped during compaction

## Justification

Document deletion is a basic operation that currently requires an expensive full rebuild. Specific consumer needs:
- **mcp_agent_mail_rust**: message archival with TTL — messages expire continuously, requiring efficient deletion
- **xf**: users may want to remove specific tweets from search results (e.g., deleted tweets, blocked accounts)
- **cass**: old sessions may be archived/deleted to keep the search index focused on recent conversations

Soft-delete with tombstones provides O(1) deletion at the cost of slightly degraded search performance (scanning tombstoned records). Vacuum reclaims this overhead when tombstones accumulate.

## Considerations

- Tombstone persistence: the flags write is immediately durable (mmap'd file, fsync on next batch operation). Individual tombstone writes without fsync are acceptable because tombstones are idempotent — re-deleting is harmless.
- doc_id lookup: soft_delete needs to find the record by doc_id. The current FSVI format stores doc_id hashes in the record. A linear scan of hashes is O(n) but sufficient for individual deletes. For batch deletes, build a hash set first.
- Concurrent access: tombstone writes (single u16) are atomic on all platforms. Readers may see stale (non-tombstoned) records briefly, which is acceptable (eventual consistency for deletes).
- Vacuum threshold: 0.2 (20%) is a reasonable default. Lower values vacuum more often (less scan overhead but more I/O). Higher values tolerate more dead records.

## Testing

- [ ] Unit: soft_delete marks record, is_deleted returns true
- [ ] Unit: soft_delete returns false for non-existent doc_id
- [ ] Unit: soft-deleted records excluded from search results
- [ ] Unit: tombstone_count and tombstone_ratio calculations are correct
- [ ] Unit: needs_vacuum triggers at correct threshold
- [ ] Unit: vacuum removes tombstoned records, search results unchanged for non-deleted docs
- [ ] Unit: vacuum stats (records_before, records_after, bytes_reclaimed) are accurate
- [ ] Unit: batch delete correctly deletes all specified doc_ids
- [ ] Integration: delete + search + vacuum full lifecycle
- [ ] Integration: concurrent delete + search (no corruption)
- [ ] Benchmark: search overhead with various tombstone ratios (0%, 10%, 20%, 50%)","acceptance_criteria":"1. The feature is fully implemented according to this bead design, scope, and referenced files or modules.
